quiz_questions.question_text: Historically, lump sum investing beats dollar-cost averaging approximately:

options.text: 50% of the time, as conventional investing theory would suggest
90% of the time, despite what intuition might initially suggest
~66% of the time (because markets trend upward, earlier investment captures more growth)
100% of the time regardless of prevailing conditions in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: Vanguard research shows lump sum investing outperforms DCA about two-thirds of the time because markets rise more often than they fall. However, DCA reduces regret risk and may help investors who would otherwise not invest at all.

quiz_questions.question_text: Emily invests $300/month for 10 years (age 22-32) at 8%, then stops. Mark invests $300/month for 30 years (age 32-62) at 8%. At age 62, Emily has ~$478K and Mark ~$440K. Emily contributed:

options.text: $108,000 (same as Mark) according to widely held
$72,000, despite what intuition might initially
$150,000 regardless of prevailing conditions
$36,000 — one-third of Mark's $108,000 — yet has $38K more

quiz_questions.explanation: Emily: $300 x 120 months = $36,000. Mark: $300 x 360 months = $108,000. Emily invested 1/3 as much money but has more at age 62, proving that starting early is far more powerful than contributing more later.

quiz_questions.question_text: Someone who panic-sells during a 30% market crash and buys back after recovery:

options.text: Saves money by avoiding further losses, even when conditions shift significantly
Gains more by timing the market when viewed through a simplified analytical lens
Locks in losses and misses the recovery — selling low and buying high is the worst possible behavior
Breaks even over time regardless of prevailing conditions in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: Selling after a 30% drop crystalizes the loss. The market must then recover before the investor buys back, meaning they miss the recovery rally. Studies show panic sellers forfeit 2-4% annual return compared to staying invested.

quiz_questions.question_text: $100,000 at 7% return vs. 6% return over 30 years: the difference is approximately:

options.text: ~$50,000 within
~$100,000, despite what

quiz_questions.explanation: At 7%: $100,000 x 1.07^30 = ~$761,226. At 6%: $100,000 x 1.06^30 = ~$574,349. The 1% difference costs ~$187,000 over 30 years. This is why minimizing fees (which reduce your effective return) matters so much.

quiz_questions.question_text: Warren Buffett's bet that an S&P 500 index fund would beat hedge funds over 10 years resulted in:

options.text: The hedge funds winning easily regardless of prevailing conditions
Buffett canceling the bet early, especially during periods
A tie without accounting for additional influences
A decisive index fund victory (S&P 500: ~125.8% return vs. hedge funds: ~36%)

quiz_questions.explanation: From 2008-2017, the S&P 500 index fund returned 125.8% while the five hedge fund portfolios averaged roughly 36%. High fees (often 2% management + 20% of profits) dragged down hedge fund returns dramatically.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the 2008 crash, the S&P 500 dropped ~50%. By 2013 it had:

options.text: Fully recovered and hit new all-time highs — patience was rewarded
Stayed flat when considering all relevant March factors
Dropped further, even when conditions shift significantly
Still not recovered, which is a common assumption in

quiz_questions.explanation: The S&P 500 hit its bottom in March 2009 and fully recovered to pre-crisis levels by March 2013. Investors who stayed the course were made whole; those who panic-sold permanently lost money.

quiz_questions.question_text: A couple earning $100K, saving 15%, with employer match 50% up to 6% saves _____ per year and accumulates approximately _____ after 30 years at 7%.

options.text: $18K/year ($15K + $3K match), ~$1.7 million
$10K/year, ~$1 million

quiz_questions.explanation: Employee saves 15% of $100K = $15,000. Employer matches 50% of 6% = $3,000. Total: $18,000/year ($1,500/month). At 7% for 30 years: FV annuity gives ~$1,700,000. The employer match adds roughly $300,000 of that total.

quiz_questions.question_text: The 'coffee cup' example: $5/day invested at 8% for 40 years grows to approximately:
quiz_questions.explanation: $5/day = ~$150/month. At 8% for 40 years (480 months): FV annuity ≈ $530,000. Total contributed: only $72,000. This shows that even a small daily expense redirected to investing can build significant wealth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Tax-advantaged Roth IRA vs. taxable account at the same return after 30 years:

options.text: Same outcome since the return is identical
Roth grows much more because there is no annual tax drag on dividends or capital gains
Taxable account is always better due to no contribution limits
The difference is negligible

quiz_questions.explanation: In a taxable account, annual dividends and realized gains reduce the compounding balance. A Roth IRA lets the full balance compound tax-free. Over 30 years, the tax drag can cost 15-25% of potential wealth.

quiz_questions.question_text: The fundamental equation of wealth building is:

options.text: Borrow to invest for maximum leverage
Invest everything regardless of expenses
Spend more, earn more
Earn - Spend = Savings; Invest wisely over time; Compound interest builds wealth

quiz_questions.explanation: Wealth building requires three steps: earn income, control spending to create savings, then invest those savings wisely. Time and compound interest do the heavy lifting, but the process starts with the discipline to save.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'survivorship bias' in fund performance data?

options.text: When failed or closed funds are excluded from performance databases, making the average fund appear to perform better than it actually does
The bias toward investing in established companies
The tendency for only surviving investors to be counted in return data
The advantage of being a long-term investor

quiz_questions.explanation: When poorly performing mutual funds are closed or merged, they disappear from databases. The remaining funds look better on average, creating a false impression that active management outperforms more often than it does.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'behavioral finance' study?

options.text: The optimal trading strategies for day traders
How psychological biases and emotional responses lead investors to make irrational decisions that harm their returns
The behavior of stock prices during market hours
The mathematical models behind stock pricing

quiz_questions.explanation: Behavioral finance identifies cognitive biases like loss aversion (feeling losses twice as strongly as gains), recency bias (overweighting recent events), and herd mentality (following the crowd) that cause investors to buy high and sell low.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'loss aversion' mean in investing?

options.text: The tax benefit of harvesting investment losses
Avoiding all investments to prevent any possibility of loss
The psychological tendency to feel the pain of losses roughly twice as strongly as the pleasure of equivalent gains, often leading to poor decisions
A strategy of stopping losses at a predetermined percentage

quiz_questions.explanation: Loss aversion causes investors to sell winners too early (to lock in gains) and hold losers too long (to avoid admitting a loss). It also drives panic selling during market crashes, the single most destructive investment behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'recency bias' in investing?

options.text: Preferring recently IPO'd stocks when considering all relevant become factors
Only investing in companies less than 5 years old, even when conditions shift significantly
Checking your portfolio too frequently, which is a common assumption in the field
Overweighting recent events and assuming current trends will continue, leading to buying at peaks and selling at bottoms

quiz_questions.explanation: After a bull market, investors become overly optimistic and invest aggressively (buying high). After a crash, they become overly pessimistic and sell (selling low). This recency-driven cycle is the opposite of rational behavior.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'total return' include?

options.text: Only the change in stock price
Both price appreciation (capital gains) and income (dividends and interest) combined
Only dividend income
Returns after subtracting all fees and taxes

quiz_questions.explanation: Total return = Capital Gains + Dividends/Interest. Ignoring dividends dramatically understates historical stock returns. Reinvested dividends account for roughly 40% of total S&P 500 returns since 1930.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'disposition effect'?

options.text: The tendency to increase investment amounts during bull markets
The tendency to dispose of all investments at retirement
Reducing portfolio holdings as you age
The tendency to sell winning investments too quickly and hold losing investments too long, driven by loss aversion

quiz_questions.explanation: The disposition effect leads investors to take small gains quickly (selling winners) while refusing to sell losers (hoping they will recover). This is the opposite of the rational strategy of letting winners run and cutting losers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sequence of returns risk' mean for retirees?

options.text: The order in which different asset classes perform each year
The risk of inflation during retirement
The risk of choosing investments in the wrong sequence
The risk that poor returns in the early years of retirement, combined with withdrawals, can permanently deplete a portfolio

quiz_questions.explanation: A major crash in the first years of retirement forces selling at low prices to fund withdrawals, permanently reducing the portfolio. The same crash later in retirement is less damaging because fewer years of withdrawals remain.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the 'wealth effect' describe?

options.text: A guaranteed increase in wealth from investing
When rising portfolio values make people feel richer and spend more, even if they haven't sold any investments
The compounding effect of reinvested dividends
The tendency for wealthy individuals to invest more

quiz_questions.explanation: The wealth effect is a behavioral phenomenon where paper gains increase consumer confidence and spending. It can lead to overconfidence in bull markets, causing people to take on more risk or save less than they should.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'anchoring bias' in investment decisions?

options.text: Anchoring your portfolio to a specific benchmark index
Investing only in well-anchored, stable companies
Being anchored to a long-term investment strategy
Fixating on a specific reference point (like the purchase price) and making decisions based on it rather than current fundamentals

quiz_questions.explanation: An investor who bought at $100 and refuses to sell at $80 'until it gets back to $100' is anchoring. The original purchase price is irrelevant to future prospects. Decisions should be based on current value and outlook, not past prices.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'herd mentality' mean in investing?

options.text: Following the crowd into or out of investments, often buying after prices have already risen and selling after they have already fallen
A conservative investment strategy, even when conditions shift significantly
Investing exclusively in livestock commodities under most circumstances involving buying
A type of agricultural investment fund, even when conditions shift significantly

quiz_questions.explanation: Herd mentality drove the dot-com bubble (everyone buying tech stocks in 1999) and the panic of 2008 (everyone selling at once). Following the crowd typically means buying high and selling low — the opposite of a winning strategy.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor paying 1.5% in fees vs. 0.1% on a $500,000 portfolio over 25 years (7% gross return) loses approximately how much to fees?
quiz_questions.explanation: At 6.9% net: $500K grows to ~$2,620K. At 5.5% net: $500K grows to ~$1,890K. Difference: ~$730K. The 1.4% fee difference compounds over 25 years to consume over half a million dollars that should have been the investor's.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two retirees each have $1,000,000 at age 65 and withdraw $40,000/year. Retiree A experiences 15% returns in years 1-3, then -10% in years 4-6. Retiree B gets -10% in years 1-3, then 15% in years 4-6. Same average return, but Retiree B:

options.text: Has a higher balance than Retiree A, based on conventional assumptions about Retiree
Has the same balance as Retiree A after 6 years, particularly in the context of Retiree
Has a significantly lower balance because early losses combined with withdrawals depleted the portfolio more severely
Has exactly $1,000,000 remaining without accounting for additional influences

quiz_questions.explanation: Retiree B suffered losses while also withdrawing $40K/year, permanently reducing the base that could benefit from later gains. Retiree A's early gains provided a larger buffer against later losses. This is sequence of returns risk in action.

quiz_questions.question_text: Real estate as a primary residence investment: a home purchased for $300,000 appreciates 3% annually to ~$403,000 after 10 years. But maintenance ($3,000/yr), property taxes ($4,000/yr), insurance ($1,500/yr), and mortgage interest ($120,000 total) add up. The true net return is:

options.text: Higher than 3% because real estate always outperforms
Negative, making homeownership always a bad investment
Significantly less than 3% after accounting for all costs; the home provides housing value but is not a pure investment
3% per year (same as appreciation)

quiz_questions.explanation: Appreciation gain: $103,000. Costs: maintenance ($30K) + taxes ($40K) + insurance ($15K) + mortgage interest ($120K) = $205,000. Net: -$102,000 before tax benefits. A home provides shelter value but is not purely a financial investment.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor puts $200,000 in a target-date 2055 fund at age 30. At 8% average return, by age 65 (35 years), the investment grows to approximately:

options.text: ~$400,000, even when
~$500,000, especially during

quiz_questions.explanation: $200,000 x 1.08^35 = approximately $2,960,000. The target-date fund automatically adjusts from aggressive (90% stocks at 30) to conservative (50% stocks near retirement), managing risk along the way.

quiz_questions.question_text: The DALBAR study consistently shows that the average equity fund investor earns _____ than the S&P 500 due to behavioral mistakes.

options.text: 5% more
3-4% less annually
1% more
About the same

quiz_questions.explanation: DALBAR's annual studies show the average investor earns 3-4% less than the market annually due to poor timing — buying after rallies and selling after declines. Over 30 years, this behavior gap costs hundreds of thousands of dollars.

quiz_questions.question_text: An investor with $50,000 invested 100% in one company stock watches it drop 70% to $15,000. A diversified index fund investor with the same $50,000 sees a 30% market decline to $35,000. The concentrated investor needs a _____ gain to recover; the diversified investor needs ____%.

options.text: 100% gain; 50% gain
233% gain; 43% gain — concentration makes recovery dramatically harder
70% gain; 30% gain
Both need the same percentage gain

quiz_questions.explanation: Concentrated: $15K to $50K needs a 233% gain. Diversified: $35K to $50K needs a 43% gain. The math of losses is asymmetric — larger losses require exponentially larger gains to recover. Diversification limits the damage.

quiz_questions.question_text: A Roth IRA investor contributes $7,000/year for 45 years (age 22 to 67) at 8% average return. The final balance is approximately $2.68 million. The total amount contributed was:
quiz_questions.explanation: $7,000/year x 45 years = $315,000 contributed. The remaining ~$2,365,000 (88%) is compound growth — all completely tax-free in a Roth IRA. This case study demonstrates why maximizing Roth contributions early in your career is so powerful.

quiz_questions.question_text: During the dot-com crash (2000-2002), the NASDAQ dropped ~78%. An investor with $100,000 entirely in tech stocks saw their portfolio fall to $22,000. If they had been diversified 60/40 stocks/bonds (broad index), their portfolio would have dropped to approximately:

options.text: $22,000 (same as tech-only), at least according to standard theory
$100,000 (bonds offset all stock losses) under most circumstances involving portfolio
$50,000 because conventional analysis of portfolio suggests this would be the expected result
~$80,000 (broad stock indices fell ~30% but bonds rose, limiting the loss to ~20% on a 60/40 portfolio)

quiz_questions.explanation: The S&P 500 fell ~45% (less than NASDAQ's 78%), and bonds actually gained during this period. A 60/40 portfolio lost roughly 20% ($100K to $80K) compared to the tech investor's 78% loss ($100K to $22K). Diversification provided massive protection.

quiz_questions.question_text: A study of three investors from 1980-2020 (40 years). Investor A: worst market timer (invested $10K each year at the market peak). Investor B: best market timer (invested at the yearly low). Investor C: invested on January 1 each year regardless. At the end:

options.text: Investor A lost money due to always buying at the peak under most circumstances involving market
All three accumulated substantial wealth; Investor B was best but Investor A (worst timer) still grew wealth significantly because time in the market overcame poor timing
Investor C performed worst, which represents a common but incomplete view of how these market dynamics operate
Only Investor B made money when the interaction between market and invested factors is taken into full account

quiz_questions.explanation: Even the worst market timer who bought at every yearly peak accumulated enormous wealth over 40 years. The difference between best and worst timing was relatively small compared to not investing at all. Consistent investing wins over timing.

quiz_questions.question_text: The biggest lesson from all investing case studies is:

options.text: Avoid the stock market entirely and stick with savings accounts
Stock picking is more important than consistent saving
The best investors perfectly time the market
Start early, invest consistently, keep costs low, diversify, and stay the course through ups and downs

quiz_questions.explanation: Every case study reinforces the same core principles: time in the market > timing the market, low fees > high fees, diversification > concentration, discipline > emotion. These simple rules, applied consistently, build substantial long-term wealth.
